revision.text:
{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and addition[[Category:addition]]al 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji ware]] and [[Sue ware]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and addition[[Category:addition]]al 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji ware]] and [[Sue ware]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and addition[[Category:addition]]al 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji ware]] and [[Sue ware]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and addition[[Category:addition]]al 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji ware]] and [[Sue ware]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and addition[[Category:addition]]al 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | Group = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and addition[[Category:addition]]al 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | other_designation = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and addition[[Category:addition]]al 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and addition[[Category:addition]]al 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and additional 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:dd]][[Category:-inline|Kashiya ancient graves}} *[https://www.town.kannami.shizuoka.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shiteibunkazai/shiseki-kunishitei_1.html Kannami Town official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kannami13.sakura.ne.jp/geo-kashiya.html Kannami Heritage Site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://izugeopark.org/geosites/kashiya/ Izu Geopark site]{{in lang|ja}}

The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and additional 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | Historic Site = | website = <!-- {{URL|example.com}} --> | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and additional 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corridor-type kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave tomb|qq=}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and additional 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:dd]][[Category:-inline|Kashiya ancient graves}} *[https://www.town.kannami.shizuoka.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shiteibunkazai/shiseki-kunishitei_1.html Kannami Town official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kannami13.sakura.ne.jp/geo-kashiya.html Kannami Heritage Site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://izugeopark.org/geosites/kashiya/ Izu Geopark site]{{in lang|ja}}
{{Kofun navbar}}

The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corridor-type kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave tomb|qq=}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and additional 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:dd]][[Category:-inline|Kashiya ancient graves}} *[https://www.town.kannami.shizuoka.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shiteibunkazai/shiseki-kunishitei_1.html Kannami Town official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kannami13.sakura.ne.jp/geo-kashiya.html Kannami Heritage Site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://izugeopark.org/geosites/kashiya/ Izu Geopark site]{{in lang|ja}}

The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[Meiji period]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and additional 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Kannami, Shizuoka|Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Kannami, Shizuoka|Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corridor-type kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave tomb|qq=}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[:en:Kano River|Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[Mount Fuji]], or [[:en:Mount Hakone|Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and additional 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:dd]][[Category:-inline|Kashiya ancient graves}} *[https://www.town.kannami.shizuoka.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shiteibunkazai/shiseki-kunishitei_1.html Kannami Town official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kannami13.sakura.ne.jp/geo-kashiya.html Kannami Heritage Site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://izugeopark.org/geosites/kashiya/ Izu Geopark site]{{in lang|ja}}

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Kannami, Shizuoka|Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Kannami, Shizuoka|Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corridor-type kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave tomb|qq=}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[:en:Kano River|Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], or [[:en:Mount Hakone|Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[:en:Nippon University|Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and additional 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[tortoise]] [[:en:plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:dd]][[Category:-inline|Kashiya ancient graves}} *[https://www.town.kannami.shizuoka.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shiteibunkazai/shiseki-kunishitei_1.html Kannami Town official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kannami13.sakura.ne.jp/geo-kashiya.html Kannami Heritage Site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://izugeopark.org/geosites/kashiya/ Izu Geopark site]{{in lang|ja}}

{{Short description|Archaeological site in Kannami, Tōkai, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Kashiya Cave Tombs | native_name =柏谷横穴群 | native_name_lang = | image =柏谷横穴群.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Kashiya Yokoana-kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{coord|35|04|58|N|138|57|34|E|display=inline, title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[:en:Kannami, Shizuoka|Kannami, Shizuoka]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]] | region = [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] | type = kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access =Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Kashiya Cave Tombs'''|柏谷横穴群| Kashiya Yokoana-kofun gun}} is an [[:en:Archaeological site|Archaeological site]] containing the ruins of a final [[Kofun period]] to early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] necropolis in the Kashiya neighborhood of the town of [[:en:Kannami, Shizuoka|Kannami, Shizuoka]] in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The site was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1976, with the area under protection extended in 1998<ref>{{cite web |url= http://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/201816 |title=柏谷横穴群 |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}}</ref> The site is also popularly known as the {{nihongo|'''Kashiya One Hundred Holes'''|柏谷の百穴|Kashiya no Hyakuketsu}}.
==Overview== The Kashiya site is the largest collection of {{nihongo|{{ill|corridor-type kofun|lt=cave tomb|kk=-type ''kofun''|ja|横穴墓|1=Cave tomb|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corridor-type kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Cave tomb|qq=}}|横穴墓|Yokōbo}} in Shizuoka Prefecture, and is located on the eastern side of the Tagata Plain in the [[:en:Kano River|Kano River]] basin at the northern end of the [[:en:Izu Peninsula|Izu Peninsula]]. The tombs are located on a steep hill and are dug into the [[:en:Tuff|Tuff]] layer in four to five layers. Some of the upper level tombs are heavily weathered, and some of the lowest level tombs are buried. The tombs have rectangular, sometimes square, openings with vaulted ceilings averaging 1.5 meters heigh, and are between two and three meters deep. The passages are filled with river stones. <ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=43dd750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The site consisted of between 300 and 500 tombs in a 600 by 250 meter area at an elevation of 20 to 30 meters, and are orientated south, east or west, facing the Tagata Plains, [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]], or [[:en:Mount Hakone|Mount Hakone]]. Per a study by the [[:en:Nippon University|Nippon University]] in 1947, the tombs can be divided into five distinct groups based on location and design, which were labelled "A" through "E".<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site has been known since at least the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]; however, most of groups "A", "D" and "E" have been destroyed by property development and modern housing. The preserved area consists of groups "B" and C" with approximately dd6 tombs. As a result of the Kannami Town Board of Education survey in 1997, and additional 17 unopened tombs were discovered, leading to the possibility that more may exist both within and without the protected area.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
[[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] recovered from the tombs include shards of [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] earthenware, gold, copper and stone jewelry (including ''[[magatama]]'' and cylindrical beads), weapons and horse fittings. One of the finds was a [[:en:Tortoise|Tortoise]] [[:en:plastrons|plastron]] used as an [[oracle bone]] for divination purposes. The graves are thought to have been created from the end of the 6th century to the end of the 8th century, and cremated human bones have also been found in the more recent graves, indicating a period of transition between burial and cremation. <ref name="Shiseki"/>
==Gallery== <gallery> Kashiwadani-oketsu5.jpg|restored cave-tombs 柏谷横穴群造り付け石棺.JPG|interior of cave-tomb KashiyayokoanaBchiku.jpg|cave-tomb in unrestored state Kashiwadani-oketsu2.jpg|Region added to National Historic Site in 1998 Kashiyayokoana2goukara6gou.jpg|backfilled grave-tombs for preservation </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Shizuoka)]]
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{commons Category:dd]][[Category:-inline|Kashiya ancient graves}} *[https://www.town.kannami.shizuoka.jp/bunka/bunkazai/shiteibunkazai/shiseki-kunishitei_1.html Kannami Town official site] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://kannami13.sakura.ne.jp/geo-kashiya.html Kannami Heritage Site] {{in lang|ja}} *[https://izugeopark.org/geosites/kashiya/ Izu Geopark site]{{in lang|ja}}
